claimed_contribution: The paper identifies general self-confidence as an antecedent of preference uncertainty in context-dependent choice.
It proposes and tests a metacognitive mechanism in which confidence from self-perceived attractiveness is misattributed to unrelated choices.
It demonstrates that marketing actions that boost perceived attractiveness, such as appearance compliments, can shift choices outside beauty categories.
context: The studies examine consumer choices in non-beauty domains such as course and roommate selection, vacation options, candy, chocolate, and pens. Evidence comes from lab and field settings in the United States, China, and Australia, including a field interaction where participants were complimented on appearance.
design_and_data: Six randomized studies were conducted with students and public participants (study Ns: 127, 65, 172, 150, 215, and 156). Self-perceived attractiveness was manipulated via writing about things that make one feel attractive, appearance-related compliments in a field study, and downward social comparison from viewing unattractive same-gender faces. Outcomes were choices designed to test compromise, all-average, and default effects, including consequential choices and real purchases (chocolate bars, pens, and a candy-bar gift). The authors measured self-confidence, mood, and in Study 5 preference uncertainty via confidence-interval width around attribute-importance ratings, and used mediation, serial mediation, and an attribution condition to test process.
main_findings: Participants who received a boost in self-perceived attractiveness reported higher general self-confidence than control participants.

Boosting self-perceived attractiveness reduced choice shares of compromise, all-average, and default options, and this pattern appeared in both hypothetical and consequential or real purchase decisions.

Process tests indicated that higher self-confidence reduced preference uncertainty, and the effect attenuated when participants attributed their confidence to feeling attractive, supporting a misattribution account.
one_line_summary: Across six experiments, boosting consumers' self-perceived attractiveness increased general self-confidence, lowered inferred preference uncertainty, and reduced context-dependent selection of compromise, all-average, and default options in unrelated choice domains.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior context-effects and preference-uncertainty research by showing that self-perceived attractiveness can alter compromise, all-average, and default choices through changes in general self-confidence. It also links metacognitive information and misattribution accounts to these consumer choice effects.
research_gap: Prior work discusses consequences of attractiveness but offers little insight into how self-perceived attractiveness affects consumer decision processes in domains unrelated to beauty. The authors also argue that antecedents of preference uncertainty are underexplored, particularly whether general self-confidence can drive context effects.
research_question: Does boosting consumers' self-perceived physical attractiveness influence subsequent choices in domains unrelated to beauty? Specifically, the paper tests whether increased self-perceived attractiveness raises general self-confidence, lowers preference uncertainty, and thereby reduces reliance on contextual cues such as compromise, all-average, and default options.
